coredata.dc:title: Self-synergy-powered Ni/Fe nanocube-based cholesterol detection with dual modes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Xiluan
Mu, Hongyi
He, Yun
Guo, Kangyi
Jiang, Pengyan
Wang, Wei
Shu, Qinglei
Deng, Huangying
Liu, Ahua
Ge, Wenkai
Cheng, Shiyun
Li, Na
Cao, Zhijua
Zheng, Xiangjuan
Ai, Fanrong
Guo, Liang
coredata.dc:description: Nanoenzyme-leveraged multimode detection would benefit enhancing sensitivity and mitigating detection error. Moreover, multienzyme-like nanozymes hold tremendous potential in sensing by offering synergistic effects and cascaded catalysis. Herein, cost-effective multienzymic Ni/Fe nanocubes (Ni/FeNCs) were synthesized via a facile co-precipitation, and verified to catalyze H2O2 decomposition as peroxidase (POD) and catalase (CAT) mimics. Thereby, a dual-mode sensing platform based on Ni/FeNC and cholesterol oxidase (ChOx) was developed for cholesterol detection. Utilizing the H2O2 produced via the oxidation of cholesterol catalyzed by ChOx, OH•/O2 •- radicals and O2 were formed efficiently via Ni/FeNCs-based H2O2 decomposition, facilitating the generation of chemiluminescence (CL) and fluorescence signals. For CL assay, an Ni/FeNC-luminol-H2O2 CL system was fabricated, where both POD-mimic-mediated radical decomposition of H2O2 and ferricyanide ions in Ni/FeNCs could induce CL reaction with respective mechanism. Notably, these two CL processes were both deduced to be enhanced by in-situ generated O2. This dual-catalyzed luminol CL system, involving self-cascade catalysis of ferricyanogen and CAT mimic as well as the self-synergy between POD-like and CAT-like activities of Ni/FeNCs, was proposed for the first time, and able to boost CL signal. To generate fluorescent signal, o-phenylenediamine was introduced, and oxidized by both OH•/O2 •- and O2 produced via POD/CAT-mimic-mediated H2O2 decomposition to 2,3-diaminophenazinc, which could quench the fluorescence of WS2 quantum dots via internal filtration effect. The Ni/FeNC-based dual-mode assay is applicable and flexible for cholesterol detection. Particularly, the low-cost Ni/FeNC is a promising candidate of luminol-H2O2 CL system due to its dual-CL-mechanism involving self-cascade and synergistic catalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ni/FeNCs
Self-cascade
Self-synergy
Dual-mode assay
Cholesterol

CL mechanism and chemiluminescent detection of cholesterol

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (82360857, 32460623, 31960207); The Science Fund for Discipline Leaders of Ganpo Juncai Distinguished Scholars of Jiangxi Province (No.20243BCE51006); Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province, China (20232BAB205087, 20212BAB206093 and 20232BAB203025); The Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars of Jiangxi Province (No. 20224ACB214005); Jiangxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Agrofood Safety and Quality (2024SSY04192); Key Research and Development Program of Jiangxi Province (No. 20232BBE50017).

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (82360857, 32460623, 31960207); The Science Fund for Discipline Leaders of Ganpo Juncai Distinguished Scholars of Jiangxi Province in 2024 (No.20243BCE51006); The Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars of Jiangxi Province (No. 20224ACB214005); Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province (20224BAB216116, 20232BAB205087, 20212BAB206093 and 20232BAB203025); Jiangxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Agrofood Safety and Quality (2024SSY04192); Jiangxi Province Key Research and Development Plan (No. 20232BBE50017).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of Ni/FeNC. (A) TEM image of Ni/FeNCs; (B) Magnified TEM image of individual Ni/FeNC, (Insert) Electron diffraction pattern; (C) XRD pattern; (D) FTIR spectrum; (E) Full XPS spectrum; (F) EDS point analysis; (G) TEM/HAADF-STEM images and corresponding elemental mapping for Fe, Ni, C, and N.
The ESR spectra of the (A) DMPO-Ni/FeNCs aqueous suspension, (B) DMPO-Ni/FeNCs methanol solution, and (C) TEMP-Ni/FeNCs aqueous suspension.

Illustration of enhancement effect in Ni/FeNCs-luminol-H2O2 CL system. (A) Self-cascade catalysis of [Fe (CN)6]3- and Ni/FeNC as catalase mimic (CAT); (B) Self-synergy of peroxidase-like (POD) and CAT-like activities of Ni/FeNC.
CL mechanisms of Ni/FeNCs-luminol-H2O2 system. (A) Ferricyanide ion-induced CL; (B) OH•/O2 •- radical-induced CL.

Ni/FeNC-based CL detection of cholesterol. (A) CL intensities of different luminol-H2O2 CL systems (Mix included cholesterol, ChOx, luminol and Ni/FeNCs); (B) Linear correlation between cholesterol concentrations and CL intensity; (C) Comparison of CL responses to cholesterol (400 μM) and different interferents (1 mM), including glutamate, cysteine, arginine, glycine, glucose and magnesium chloride.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of WS2 QDs at different excitation wavelengths; (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra of WS2 QDs (red), DAP (5 mM) (black) and WS2 QDs/DAP mixture (blue); (C) Fluorescence excitation (red) and emission spectra of WS2 QDs (black); Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectrum of DAP (blue); (D) Lifetimes of WS2 QDs in the absence (black) and presence (red) of DAP. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Ni/FeNC-based fluorescent detection of cholesterol. (A) Feasibility test of Ni/FeNC-based fluorescence quenching of WS2 QDs by using OPD and H2O2; (B) Fluorescence spectra of WS2 QDs in the presence of different concentrations of cholesterol (Arrow indicates the increase of concentrations from 50 μM to 475 μM); (C) Fluorescence response at 414 nm to different concentrations of cholesterol (50 μM–475 μM); (D) Comparison of fluorescence responses to cholesterol (300 μM) and different interferents (500 μM), including glutamate, cysteine, arginine, glycine, glucose and magnesium chloride.
Ni/FeNC-based CL and fluorescence detections of cholesterol.

head.ce:title: Self-synergy-powered Ni/Fe nanocube-based cholesterol detection with dual modes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanoenzyme-leveraged multimode detection would benefit enhancing sensitivity and mitigating detection error. Moreover, multienzyme-like nanozymes hold tremendous potential in sensing by offering synergistic effects and cascaded catalysis. Herein, cost-effective multienzymic Ni/Fe nanocubes (Ni/FeNCs) were synthesized via a facile co-precipitation, and verified to catalyze H2O2 decomposition as peroxidase (POD) and catalase (CAT) mimics. Thereby, a dual-mode sensing platform based on Ni/FeNC and cholesterol oxidase (ChOx) was developed for cholesterol detection. Utilizing the H2O2 produced via the oxidation of cholesterol catalyzed by ChOx, OH•/O2 •- radicals and O2 were formed efficiently via Ni/FeNCs-based H2O2 decomposition, facilitating the generation of chemiluminescence (CL) and fluorescence signals. For CL assay, an Ni/FeNC-luminol-H2O2 CL system was fabricated, where both POD-mimic-mediated radical decomposition of H2O2 and ferricyanide ions in Ni/FeNCs could induce CL reaction with respective mechanism. Notably, these two CL processes were both deduced to be enhanced by in-situ generated O2. This dual-catalyzed luminol CL system, involving self-cascade catalysis of ferricyanogen and CAT mimic as well as the self-synergy between POD-like and CAT-like activities of Ni/FeNCs, was proposed for the first time, and able to boost CL signal. To generate fluorescent signal, o-phenylenediamine was introduced, and oxidized by both OH•/O2 •- and O2 produced via POD/CAT-mimic-mediated H2O2 decomposition to 2,3-diaminophenazinc, which could quench the fluorescence of WS2 quantum dots via internal filtration effect. The Ni/FeNC-based dual-mode assay is applicable and flexible for cholesterol detection. Particularly, the low-cost Ni/FeNC is a promising candidate of luminol-H2O2 CL system due to its dual-CL-mechanism involving self-cascade and synergistic catalysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantitative detection of biomarkers relative to disease is essential for diagnosis and therapy. The previous biomarker assays are mainly realized by employing single-mode sensors, such as CL assay, colorimetric assay, fluorescence analysis, electrochemical methods, etc. However, single-signal output sensors sometimes suffer from large detection error, whereas multi-mode sensors can utilize data from different modes to obtain more comprehensive information for improving the accuracy and robustness of quantification, reducing false and missed detection rates, broadening dynamic detection range, and adapting to diverse practical laboratory situations. Thus, multimode analysis is an feasible strategy to realize reliable detection, particularly in the field of disease diagnosis [1,2]. To date, various multi-mode sensors have been reported, such as colorimetric-fluorescent [3,4], CL-colorimetric [5,6], colorimetric-photothermal dual-signal sensors [7,8], and even triple-signal sensors [9].

Enzymatic signal amplification has been widely applied to improve detection sensitivity in the fields of biosensing, immunoassay, diagnosis and therapy, and environmental monitoring. Nevertheless, the disadvantages such as high cost, low stability and easy inactivation limit the practical applications of natural enzyme. Nanozymes are the latest generation of artificial enzyme mimics, which are de-fined as nanomaterials with inherent enzyme-like catalytic properties [10,11]. Due to their excellent stability, high pH/temperature tolerance, adjustable catalytic property, and easy-to-mass-produce, nanozymes are ideal substitutes for natural enzymes in a wide range of applications [12]. Among them, peroxidase mimetic nanozymes have been applied in constructing sensors base on diverse modes because the radicals produced by catalytical reaction can trigger the generation of various signals [13]. Thus, peroxidase mimetic nanozymes are potential candidates for the fabrication of multimode sensors.

In addition, tandem reactions integrating sequential enzyme reactions have been widely explored to match some complex processes in sensing, therapy, multi-step synthesis and environmental remediation. Taking the above-mentioned advantages of nanozyme, nanozyme-involved tandem catalysis emerged and became a promising catalysis system in various fields recently [14–16]. Nanozyme-involved tandem or cascade catalysis can be catalyzed by hybrid nanozyme, individual nanozyme with multi-enzyme catalytic activities [17], or catalyst combination of natural enzyme and nanozyme. Among them, multienzyme-like nanozymes offering self-cascade catalysis and synergistic effects based on individual nanozyme is more-favored for avoiding the assembly of various (nano)enzymes and enhancing the compactness of catalytic reactions [18]. In biosensing applications, natural-enzyme-nanozyme combination strategy have been applied generally due to the highly specific recognition ability of natural enzyme, which can enhance analytical specificity as well as signal intensity [19–21]. Therefore, the combined application of multienzyme-like nanozyme and natural enzyme holds great potential in the development of specific and sensitive analysis.

Transition metal hexacyanoferrate (MHCF) is a class of coordination compound with unique physical and chemical properties, such as wide migration channel and large number of ion sites in framework [22,23]. Due to its obviously electrocatalytic redox activity and high electron transfer rate, the analytical applications of MHCF nanoparticles have been focused on electrochemical sensing. For examples, Prussian blue [24], nickel-iron hexacyanoferrate [25], copper-iron hexacyanoferrate [26] and potassium cobalt hexacyanoferrate [27] nanoparticles were utilized to modify electrodes for electrochemical sensing of hydrogen peroxide, hydrazine and Lactobacillus rhamnosus, respectively. Meanwhile, colorimetric assays involving MHCF also have been reported [28], while their sensitivities were relatively low, which limited its application. However, few MHCF-based sensor with detection modes other than electrochemical and colorimetric sensing have been reported. Compared with noble metal based nanozyme, MHCF nanoparticles are more attractive enzyme mimics in certain respects due to their low cost, and environmentally friendly and facile synthesis procedure.

Cholesterol, one of lipid molecules, is essential for several key cellular processes. It is not only the sole precursor of steroid hormones but also an important component of cell membranes [29–31]. However, it is also associated with many diseases, such as coronary heart disease, hypertension, atherosclerosis, Alzheimer's disease, and cancer [30]. If too much cholesterol is consumed, high blood cholesterol and even cholesterol crystals can be produced. The cholesterol crystals in the bloodstream easily penetrate blood vessels, and induce cerebral hemorrhage, coronary heart disease and stroke [32]. There-fore, it is necessary to develop a convenient, inexpensive, and reliable method for cholesterol detection. To date, many assays have been applied for cholesterol detection, such as electrochemistry [33,34], electrochemiluminescence [35], fluorescence [36,37], CL [38], and colorimetric methods [29,39,40]. Nevertheless, these strategies are essentially limited by the unitary detection mode, which is susceptible to potential interference, resulting in inaccuracy. Moreover, the sensor with single mode cannot work in certain practical scenarios. To address this issue, it is necessary to develop multimode cholesterol assays for improving detection accuracy and flexibility [41].
Detailed information of the reagents and apparatus are presented in the Supporting Information.

The Ni/FeNCs were prepared by co-precipitation method. Solution A was formed by adding 1 mmol NiCl2, 2 mmol of K2C2O4 into 200 mL of deionized (DI) water. 1 mmol K3[Fe(CN)6], 2 mmol KCl, and 1.5 g of PVP-K30 were dissolved in 200 mL of ultrapure water and stirred homogeneously to form solution B. Then, solution A was added into solution B dropwise in 12 h by peristaltic pump, and stirred for 24 h. The precipitated Ni/FeNCs particles were collected by filtration and washed several times with DI water. Finally, the Ni/FeNCs were vacuum-dried at 60 °C for over 12 h.

WS2 QDs were synthesized according to the reported method with some modifications [42]. 0.05 g of Na2WO4·H2O was added to 10 mL DI water, and the pH was adjusted to 6.0 by adding 0.1 M HCl. Then this solution and 0.69 g reduced glutathione (GSH) were dissolved in 40 mL DI water. Finally, the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, which was placed in the oven and heated at 200 °C for 9 h. Then the prepared WS2 QDs were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CL and fluorometric detection of cholesterol
ce:section.ce:para: CL assay: (a) 10 μL ChOx solution (0.2 U mL−1 in pure water) and 100 μL cholesterol solution with different concentrations (containing 0.8 % TritonX-100) were mixed and reacted for 20 min at 37 °C. (b) 30 μL of commercial luminol solution and 30 μL of Ni/FeNCs suspension (0.9 mg L−1) were added to the above mixture. (c) Monitor the CL intensity.

Fluorometric assay: (a) 100 μL ChOx solution (0.2 U mL−1 in pure water) and cholesterol solution with different concentrations (containing 0.8 % TritonX-100) were mixed and reacted for 20 min at 37 °C. (b) 30 μL Ni/FeNCs suspension (0.6 g L−1), 200 μL WS2 QDs (stock solution) and 50 μL OPD (5 mM) were added to the above mixture. (c) Reaction in incubated shaker for 20 min at 37 °C. (d) Monitor the fluorescence intensity at 414 nm.

The serum samples were diluted 100-fold and 50-fold with ultrapure water for CL and fluorometric assays, respectively. To validate the feasibility of the proposed CL assay, the 100-fold diluted serum samples were fortified by adding different volumes of cholesterol stock solutions (1 mM) to produce spiked samples with various concentrations for subsequential CL detections. Similarly, to validate the feasibility of the proposed fluorometric assay, the 50-fold diluted serum samples were fortified by adding different volumes of stock solutions to produce spiked samples with various cholesterol concentrations for subsequential assays.

A yellow powder of Ni/FeNCs was prepared via a facile co-precipitation method followed by vacuum drying (Fig. S1). The morphology and size of the synthesized Ni/FeNCs were examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The results in Fig. 1 A and B show that Ni/FeNCs exhibit cubic structure with an average particle size of 80 nm. The electron diffraction pattern (Fig. 1B Insert) demonstrates the crystalline nature of Ni/FeNCs. To further confirm the crystallinity, Ni/FeNCs were subjected to X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) analysis. Several sharp diffraction peaks in the XRD patterns were located at 17.4°, 24.7°, 35.3°, 39.6°, 43.7°, 50.7°, 54.1°, and 57.3° (Fig. 1C), which were in general consistent with previous report [43]. As seen in Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra of Ni/FeNCs (Fig. 1D), the peaks present at 3406 cm−1 and 1640 cm−1 are attributed to the O–H stretching vibration in H2O, respectively. The sharp peak present at 2098 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of the CN bond of cyanide coordinated with Fe and Ni [25,44]. Then X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to further check the oxidation state of Ni/Fe in the prepared Ni/FeNCs. Fig. 1E shows the measured XPS spectra in the range 0–1200 eV, which confirms the presence of Ni, Fe, C and N elements in Ni/FeNCs. The high-resolution XPS spectra of Ni are shown in Fig. S2A, where the peaks at 855.5 eV and 872.8 eV correspond to Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 of Ni3+, while the two peaks at binding energies of 856.3 eV and 873.9 eV belong to Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 of Ni2+, respectively. In the high-resolution spectrum of Fe 2p (Fig. S2B), the peaks at 708.2 eV and 721.8 eV are assigned to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 of Fe2+, while the peaks at 710.2 eV and 723.9 eV attributed to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 of Fe3+, respectively. It confirmed that Fe element existed in the form of Fe(III) and Fe(II), guaranteeing its high catalytic behavior on the CL reaction. The N 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S2C) can be deconvoluted into a peak at 397.3 eV, corresponding to the cyanide in Ni/FeNCs (CN). Energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) point analysis (Fig. 1F) further demonstrates the presence of C, N, O, K, Fe and Ni elements in the material. Moreover, the TEM elemental mapping shows that Ni, Fe, N and C distributed uniformly in the Ni/Fe NCs (Fig. G). All these results confirmed the successful synthesis of nickel ferrocyanide nanocubes.

Radicals can induce the CL generation of luminol-H2O2 system. In order to clarify the catalytic mechanism of Ni/FeNCs, free radical scavengers were applied to capture radicals produced in the luminol–H2O2–Ni/FeNCs CL system. Ascorbic acid (multiple free radical inhibitors), thiourea (OH• inhibitor), tryptophan (O2 •- scavenger), and hydroxylammonium chloride (1O2 quencher) were used to shed light on the role of the corresponding reactive oxygen species [45,46]. In the presence of a scavenger, the CL signal would be reduced if the catalytic system produced its corresponding active substances. As shown in Fig. S3, hydroxyl radicals (OH•) and superoxide ions (O2 •-) played a major role in the reaction. To further verify the radicals generated during the H2O2 decomposition process, we performed electron spin resonance (ESR) spectral analysis with 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrrolidine-n-oxide (DMPO) which specifically capturing OH• and O2 •-, and with 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) which specifically capturing 1O2. The results in Fig. 2 A shows that the peak intensity increased significantly with a peak intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 after the addition of Ni/FeNCs to the aqueous system with DMPO, demonstrating that Ni/FeNCs can induce the radical decomposition of H2O2 to produce OH•. Fig. 2B presents four enhanced peaks with an intensity of 1:1:1:1 when Ni/FeNCs was dispersed in methanol system with DMPO, which proved the generation of O2 •- [47]. As seen in Fig. 2C, the peak intensity did not change significantly after adding Ni/FeNCs to the aqueous system with TEMP, revealing that almost no 1O2 was produced during the Ni/FeNCs catalyzed H2O2 activation. Therefore, it was OH• and O2 •- rather than 1O2 that contributed significantly to the CL reaction catalyzed by Ni/FeNC through its POD-like activity.

In addition, substantial gas bubbles were observed when Ni/FeNCs were added to aqueous H2O2 solution (Fig. S4 and supplementary video), revealing that Ni/FeNCs could catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 into oxygen via CAT-like activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CL mechanism and chemiluminescent detection of cholesterol
ce:section.ce:para: According to the aforementioned catalytic activities of Ni/FeNCs, the CL mechanism of Ni/FeNCs-luminol-H2O2 system was deduced. It has been reported that ferricyanide ions could catalyze the CL reaction between luminol and dissolved oxygen in H2O [48]. However, the CL intensity of ferricyanide ion-luminol-H2O2 system was quite low ascribed to its low oxygen concentration (Fig. S5). Benefiting from the CAT-like activity of Ni/FeNCs, O2 was in-situ generated around the ferricyanide ions in Ni/FeNCs via catalytical H2O2 decomposition. The self-supplied O2 could participate in the ferricyanide ion-induced CL reaction by reacting with the unstable intermediate formed through the catalyzation of ferricyanide ion (Fig. 3 A). Therefore, the catalysis of ferricyanide ion in Ni/FeNCs and the CAT-like activity of Ni/FeNCs were integrated to form a self-cascade catalysis. Such ingeniously self-cascaded catalysis would boost the ferricyanide ion-induced CL reactions efficiently by highly increasing the local concentration of O2, reducing mass transfer resistance, and minimizing intermediate decomposition, which made it much more efficient than regular [Fe (CN)6]3--H2O-luminol CL system. To our knowledge, it is the first time to propose such a self-cascade catalysis of [Fe (CN)6]3- and CAT mimic in CL system.

Fig. 4A and Fig. S6A depict the CL reaction steps induced by ferricyanide ions in details. Firstly, luminol in an alkaline medium interacted with OH− ions to produce luminol dianion, which was transformed to unstable semidione derivative of luminol subsequently through the catalysis of ferricyanide ions. Then, the unstable semidione derivative was oxidized by the in-situ generated oxygen to α-hydroxy peroxide anion with an intermediate of peroxyl radical. Finally, α-hydroxy peroxide anion decomposed to excited state 3-amino phthalic acid ions (3-APA∗), accompanying the light emission at 425 nm.

On the other hand, Ni/FeNC-based CL also could be induced by OH• and O2 •- via another mechanism owing to the radical decomposition of H2O2 catalyzed by Ni/FeNC with its POD-like activity (Fig. 3B). According to the previous studies [49,50], OH•/O2 •--induced CL mechanism involving three reaction pathways was deduced and detailly presented in Fig. 4 B and Fig. S6B. In pathway (1), luminol was first hydrolyzed to luminol monoanion (LH−), followed by oxidation with OH• to generate luminol radical (L∗-). Then, L∗- was oxidized by O2 •- to form α-hydroxy peroxide (L−OOH). Being different from pathway (1), pathway (2) and (3) were involved in generating L−OOH with the participation of O2. In pathway (2), in-situ generated O2 could react with L∗- to supply additional O2 •- which further react with L∗- to form L−OOH, while producing diazaquinone derivative of luminol (L(Daq)) as a reactant in pathway (3). In pathway (3), the afore-mentioned L(Daq) was oxidized by mono-dissociated hydrogen peroxide (HO2 −) and produced L−OOH. Finally, L−OOH decomposed to 3-APA∗, accompanying the CL emission.

Hence, with the participation of O2, one L∗- was transformed to two active species (O2 •- and L(Daq)) which induced the formation of two L−OOH with consumption of another L∗- to react with O2 •- in pathway (2) and (3). Since the formation of two L∗- consumed two OH•, two L−OOH was formed with consumption of two radicals (OH•) as presented in overall reaction equation of pathway (2) and (3) (Fig. S6B). However, only one L−OOH was formed with consumption of two radicals (one OH• and one O2 •-) in pathway (1) without the participation of O2. Therefore, compared with O2-free OH•/O2 •--induced luminol CL system, the Ni/FeNC-based radical CL system could enhance CL efficiency by producing the additional L(Daq) in the reaction between L∗- and in-situ generated O2 which facilitated by the CAT-like activity of Ni/FeNCs (Fig. 3B).

In brief, the CL of Ni/FeNCs-luminol-H2O2 system can be induced by ferricyanide ion and POD-like activity of Ni/FeNCs via dual mechanism. Particularly, the ferricyanide ion-induced CL process integrated the catalysis of ferricyanide ion and CAT-like catalysis of Ni/FeNCs, resulting in a self-cascade catalysis with higher CL efficiency. Meanwhile, the OH•/O2 •--induced CL was deduced to be boosted via the self-synergy of POD-like and CAT-like activities of Ni/FeNC.

The influence of CAT-like activity of Ni/FeNCs on the CL of Ni/FeNCs-luminol-H2O2 system was investigated by purging the solution of oxygen with pure nitrogen gas and comparing the CL intensities with or without aerating reactant solution. The results in Fig. S5C shows that CL intensity decreased by 28.4 % when the reactant solution was purged with nitrogen during reaction, revealing that the CAT-like activity of Ni/FeNC facilitates CL reaction by catalyzing in-situ generation of oxygen.

It is well-known that CL assay has been widely applied for its fine sensitivity [51,52]. For CL detection of cholesterol, an aqueous ChOx-Ni/FeNC-luminol-H2O2 CL system was fabricated. Its CL response to cholesterol were investigated. The results in Fig. 5 A illustrate that a strong CL signal was generated only when cholesterol, ChOx, luminol and Ni/FeNCs all existed, demonstrating the excellent responsibility of the proposed CL system to cholesterol.

To maximize the CL response to cholesterol, the dosages of ChOx, Ni/FeNCs and luminol as well as the reaction time of ChOx catalyzed cholesterol oxidation were optimized. Firstly, different volumes (5–40 μL) of ChOx solution (0.2 U mL−1) were added to the CL system to explore the effect of ChOx dosage on CL. As shown in Fig. S7A, the maximum CL intensity was reached by using 10 μL of ChOx solution with a final concentration of 0.012 U mL−1. So, we chose 10 μL of 0.2 U mL−1 ChOx for subsequent experiments. Subsquently, the optimum catalysis time of ChOx was evaluated to be 20 min (Fig. S7B). Then, the influence of Ni/FeNCs dosage was studied by using different volumes of 0.9 mg L−1 Ni/FeNCs suspension. As illustrated in Fig. S7C, the optimal volume was 30 μL with a final Ni/FeNCs concentration of 0.16 mg L−1. Finally, we investigated the effect of luminol dosage on CL response by using different volumes of commercial luminol solution (Fig. S7D). In the range of 10–50 μL, the CL reached the maximum intensity when 30 μL of commercial luminol solution was added. Hence, 30 μL of commercial luminol solution was used in the proposed CL system.

Under optimal conditions, a good linear correlation between CL intensity and the cholesterol concentration in the range of 5∼500 μM (R
2 = 0.9902) with a detection limit of 3.35 μM were obtained (Fig. 5B). The detection limit was calculated by using 3σ/k method, where σ was the standard deviation of blank measurement and k was the slope of the calibration graph. As shown in Table S1, the sensitivity of the proposed Ni/FeNC-based CL method was superior to that of previous CL methods involving non-noble metal-based nanocomposites or natural enzyme cascade-based hydrogel, while it was inferior to that of the Au nanoparticle/luminol/soybean POD/ChOx-involved biochip which fabricated via a complicated procedure. Moreover, to evaluate the selectivity of cholesterol assay, the CL signals of potential interferences in human serum (glutamate, uric acid, urea, cysteine, arginine, glycine, glucose and magnesium chloride) were investigated. As depicted in Fig. 5C, these interferences induced negligible responses compared with cholesterol. This result demonstrated that the proposed nanozyme-based assay possessed excellent specificity toward cholesterol. Furthermore, to validate the feasibility of the CL assay for the detection of cholesterol in serum, 100-fold diluted human serum samples were spiked with different dosages of cholesterol (20–300 μM) for CL assay. The results in Table S2 showed that the average recoveries ranged from 98.0 % to 102.1 % with RSDs in the range of 3.1 %–4.7 %.

Fluorescence assay is another popular sensitive detection method ascribed to its rapid response and simplicity [53,54]. In this study, a fluorescence quenching system consisting of ChOx, Ni/FeNCs, OPD and WS2 QDs was fabricated for fluorometric detection of cholesterol. When cholesterol existed, H2O2 could be generated via catalytic oxidation of cholesterol by ChOx. Then, the decomposition of H2O2 was catalyzed by Ni/FeNCs to form OH•/O2 •- radicals and O2, which both could oxidize OPD to DAP subsequently [55,56]. Once DAP was produced, the fluorescence of WS2 QDs would be quenched via their overlapped spectra.

The influence of CAT-like activity of Ni/FeNCs on the oxidization of OPD to DAP in Ni/FeNCs–H2O2-OPD system was investigated by detecting the FL intensity of generated DAP with or without aerating with pure nitrogen to purge oxygen from the solution during reaction. As shown in Fig. S8, the FL intensity of reactant solution without nitrogen purging is 1.16 times of that with nitrogen purging, indicating that the CAT-like activity of Ni/FeNC facilitates the formation of DAP by catalyzing in-situ generation of oxygen and thereby will elevate the quenching efficiency.

Fig. 6 A displays the fluorescence emission spectrum of WS2 QDs at different excitation wavelengths. The maximum emission was obtained at the excitation wavelength of 320 nm, which was used in following experiments. To investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of WS2 QDs, DAP and the mixture of them were measured (Fig. 6B). Compared with the individual absorption spectra of WS2 QDs and DAP, no new absorption peak appeared in the spectra of their mixture, demonstrating that non-luminous ground state complex was not formed between WS2 QDs and DAP. Fig. 6C shows that the excitation and emission spectra of WS2 QDs partly overlapped with the UV–visible absorption spectra of DAP. The partly overlapped spectra indicates that the fluorescence quenching may be induced via IFE or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Due to FRET accompanies the decrease of fluorescence lifetime, while IFE accompanies negligible change, IFE can be distinguished from FRET with the change of fluorescence lifetime. As shown in Fig. 6D, the fluorescence lifetime QDs (5.48 ns) kept constant in the absence and presence of DAP, indicative of an IFE-based statistic fluorescence-quenching process of WS2 QDs.

Fig. 7 A demonstrated that the fluorescence of WS2 QDs (green curve) could be quenched when H2O2, Ni/FeNCs and OPD existed (blue curve). To maximize the fluorescence response (F0–F, ΔF) to cholesterol, the dosages of ChOx, OPD and Ni/FeNCs were optimized. The results in Fig.S9 A-C shows that fluorescence intensities decreased with the increased dosages of ChOx, OPD and Ni/FeNCs, and then reached constant values. Therefore, the best quenching efficiency was obtained when their final concentrations were 0.064 U mL−1, 0.45 mM and 32.0 mg L−1, respectively.

Fig. 7B shows that the fluorescence intensity at the emission wavelength of 414 nm declined gradually with the elevated cholesterol concentration. The dependence of ΔF on cholesterol concentration under optimal conditions was displayed in Fig. 7C. The calibration graph was linear in the range of 50–475 μM with a detection limit of 46 μM (3σ/k). The specificity of the fluorometric assay was also investigated by measuring the signal intensities of potential interferences (glutamate, cysteine, arginine, glycine, glucose and magnesium chloride). The results in Fig. 7D revealed that the proposed fluorescent assay possessed a high specificity toward cholesterol. Moreover, to validate the feasibility of the fluorescent assay for the detection of cholesterol in serum, different dosages of cholesterol were spiked to 50-fold diluted human serum samples for fluorescent assay. As shown in Table S3, the average recoveries ranged from 98.3 % to 109.8 % with RSDs in the range of 1.2 %–6.9 %, demonstrating this method feasible for the determination of cholesterol in human blood.

In summary, cost-effective Ni/Fe nanocubes with POD-like and CAT-like activities were synthesized via a facile co-precipitation method to develop a ChOx-Ni/FeNC-based CL and fluorescence sensing platform for cholesterol detection. In the proposed Ni/FeNC-luminol-H2O2 CL system, both ferricyanide ions in Ni/FeNCs and OH•/O2 •- radicals could induce CL reaction, which were deduced to be enhanced by in-situ generated O2 via self-cascade catalysis of [Fe(CN)6]3- and CAT-mimic as well as the self-synergy between POD-like and CAT-like activities of Ni/FeNCs, respectively. Meanwhile, fluorescence quenching system consisting of Ni/FeNCs, OPD and WS2 QDs was fabricated for fluorometric assay via dual-mode catalytic H2O2 decomposition and IFE. The detection limits of cholesterol via chemiluminescence and fluorescence modes were 3.35 μM and 46 μM, respectively, with linear ranges of 5–500 μM and 50–475 μM. This work presents the potential of multienzymic Ni/FeNC as a promising candidate of nanozyme-leveraged multimode detection, especially of luminol CL system due to its dual-CL-mechanism involving self-cascade and synergistic catalysis. In addition, other novel high-performance CL-related nanozymes might be inspired by the proposed CL enhancement mechanisms.

Xiluan Yan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Hongyi Mu: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Yun He: Data curation. Kangyi Guo: Data curation. Pengyan Jiang: Investigation, Data curation. Wei Wang: Investigation, Data curation. Qinglei Shu: Investigation, Data curation. Huangying Deng: Investigation, Data curation. Ahua Liu: Investigation, Data curation. Wenkai Ge: Investigation, Data curation. Shiyun Cheng: Data curation. Na Li: Data curation. Zhijua Cao: Data curation. Xiangjuan Zheng: Data curation. Fanrong Ai: Data curation. Liang Guo: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
